### Calls per Active User

Weekly call centre volume divided by the size of the active user base, so you can
compare support demand across weeks even as the user base grows.

Raw call volume moves partly because the number of people using CanadaLogin
changes. Normalizing by active users takes that out and leaves how often a typical
user needed to phone in on a given day. The figure is scaled to calls per 100
active users to keep it in a readable range.

*Alternative names*:

- call centre contact rate
- normalized call volume
- support contacts per active user
- calls per 100 users

**Calculation.** For each call centre reporting week, take `calls_accepted` from
`call_centre.weekly_activity_report` and divide it by the total user-days in that
week, then multiply by 100. User-days is the sum of
`ibm_verify.auth_total_logins.unique_users` over the days whose `date` falls
inside the week's `date_range_start` to `date_range_end`, so the numerator and
denominator cover the same span of days. This is the same as dividing average
daily calls by average daily active users. The two tables store their dates as
text, so both sides of the join are cast to `DATE`.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## SQL

```sql
SELECT
    w.date_range_start,
    w.date_range_end,
    CAST(w.calls_accepted AS BIGINT) AS calls_accepted,
    AVG(a.unique_users) AS avg_daily_active_users,
    CAST(w.calls_accepted AS DOUBLE) / SUM(a.unique_users) * 100
        AS calls_per_100_dau
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report AS w
JOIN ibm_verify.auth_total_logins AS a
    ON CAST(a.date AS DATE)
       BETWEEN CAST(w.date_range_start AS DATE) AND CAST(w.date_range_end AS DATE)
GROUP BY w.date_range_start, w.date_range_end, w.calls_accepted
ORDER BY w.date_range_start;
```

## R

```r
weekly <- tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  select(date_range_start, date_range_end, calls_accepted)

dau <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  select(dau_date = date, unique_users)

weekly |>
  inner_join(dau, by = character()) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(dau_date) >= as.Date(date_range_start),
    as.Date(dau_date) <= as.Date(date_range_end)
  ) |>
  group_by(date_range_start, date_range_end, calls_accepted) |>
  summarise(
    avg_daily_active_users = mean(unique_users, na.rm = TRUE),
    user_days = sum(unique_users, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    calls_per_100_dau = calls_accepted / user_days * 100
  ) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    calls_accepted,
    avg_daily_active_users,
    calls_per_100_dau
  )
```

:::

*Interpretation notes.* Read this as a daily rate: on a typical day that week,
roughly this many calls came in per 100 active users. Normalizing by user-days
keeps the numerator and denominator on the same daily footing, since weekly calls
divided by a single day's active users would mix a weekly count with a daily one.
The `avg_daily_active_users` column is shown for context; it is the week's average
daily reach, and dividing calls by it and then by the seven days in the week gives
the same rate. A week only appears once daily active user data exists for it:
`auth_total_logins` begins near the April 22, 2026 public launch, and the join
drops any earlier call centre week that has no matching days. The numerator is
`calls_accepted`, which includes test calls, so weeks with unusual test activity
read slightly high.
